context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Corruption and Reform of Public Institutions

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is corruption-free development ever possible? How does one understand claims of Asian distinctiveness about corruption – the 'grease the wheel' thesis – against the Asian successes in rooting out corruption as in Hong Kong? What does corruption signify about the State, its legitimacy and the ensemble of techniques through which development interventions occur?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I need help with my 700–800 word reflection on corruption and reform of public institutions. I’m kinda stuck on how to answer the “corruption-free development” part without making it too simplistic.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point to flag that concern — it shows you're already thinking critically. So let's start there: what's your instinct? Do you think corruption-free development is possible, and why or why not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting the assignment and expressing a concern about oversimplifying the first part of the prompt. Rather than scaffolding immediately, I should ask them what they currently think about the question to assess their baseline understanding and get them to engage with the problem themselves. This is a good opening Socratic move.

exchanges.student: My first instinct is prob not fully possible, at least not in a perfect sense. But I’m not sure if that sounds too cynical for the prompt.
exchanges.tutor: Don't worry about sounding cynical — worry about whether your argument holds up. So what would you point to, from the course or from what you know, that supports the idea that corruption-free development is hard to achieve?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable instinct but is second-guessing themselves on tone rather than engaging with the substance. I should push them to think about what evidence or reasoning supports their instinct, rather than worrying about whether it sounds 'cynical'. This moves the conversation toward substantive engagement with the prompt.

exchanges.student: I guess cuz the state has a lot of power and resources, so there’s always some chance of abuse. Also development has so many actors involved, esp external ones, that accountability gets messy.
exchanges.tutor: Those are solid observations that connect well to the course themes. Now, the prompt also asks you to put two seemingly contradictory ideas in tension: the 'grease the wheel' argument and Hong Kong's success in rooting out corruption. What do you know about either of those, and how do you think they relate to each other?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building a reasonable foundation connecting state power, resource control, and accountability gaps to corruption. These are substantive points worth affirming briefly. Now I should push them toward the second part of the prompt — the 'grease the wheel' thesis vs. Hong Kong — to keep the conversation moving toward a fuller response.

exchanges.student: I kinda get “grease the wheel” as corruption making rigid systems move faster, but tbh I’m fuzzy on Hong Kong beyond it being used as a success case. Can u help me connect those without acting like they cancel each other out?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — they don't simply cancel each other out. Think about this: if corruption can 'grease' a system, what does that tell you about the system itself? And what would have to change for a place like Hong Kong to successfully reduce it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student understands the 'grease the wheel' thesis at a basic level but is fuzzy on Hong Kong. Rather than explaining Hong Kong to them, I should ask a guiding question that helps them think about what conditions might allow a place to move from high corruption to low corruption. This keeps them doing the intellectual work while moving toward the tension the prompt is asking them to explore.

exchanges.student: I think it means the formal system is prob inefficient or blocked, so people use bribery as a workaround. Then Hong Kong would show that if institutions get stronger and more legit, ppl don’t need that workaround as much.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong connection — and notice you just used a key word: legitimacy. The prompt specifically asks what corruption signifies about the state and its legitimacy. Building on what you just said, what do you think corruption actually reveals about a state?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong inferential leap connecting institutional quality and legitimacy to corruption dynamics. This directly feeds into the third part of the prompt about what corruption signifies about the state and its legitimacy. I should affirm this briefly and push them toward that third dimension of the prompt now.

exchanges.student: Maybe that the state’s rules exist on paper but aren’t working fairly in practice. So corruption kinda shows weak legitimacy, or at least that citizens don’t trust normal channels to get things done.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely strong insight — corruption as a symptom of a gap between formal rules and lived reality is a sophisticated point. You've now worked through all three parts of the prompt. I think you have enough to start a first draft. What would your opening argument look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making excellent progress and has now touched on all three parts of the prompt in conversation. They've covered: corruption-free development being unlikely due to power/accountability issues; the grease-the-wheel vs. Hong Kong tension; and now what corruption signifies about state legitimacy. This is a good moment to push them toward integration — they have enough material to start writing. Per the redundancy rule and the goal of moving toward a written product, I should prompt them to start drafting.

exchanges.student: Maybe my main claim is that corruption-free development is more of an ideal than a totally reachable condition, and corruption matters because it reveals how state power, legitimacy, and institutions actually function. I’m just not sure how to phrase the Asia/Hong Kong part in the thesis rn.
exchanges.tutor: Good core claim. Now think about it this way: does the Asia/Hong Kong tension support your main argument, complicate it, or both? How does it fit into the point you're making about state power and legitimacy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid main claim forming. Rather than helping them phrase the Asia/Hong Kong part (which would cross into writing for them), I should ask them a question that helps them figure out how the Asia/HK tension fits into their overall argument. The goal is to get them to see it as evidence or a case that complicates or enriches their main claim, not a separate thread.

exchanges.student: Both imo. The “grease” idea shows corruption can emerge from rigid or weak institutions, while Hong Kong shows it’s not culturally fixed and can be reduced when the state builds credible institutions.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the kind of nuanced framing the prompt is looking for. You now have a clear argument and know how your evidence fits. It's time to stop planning and start writing. Take a shot at a full draft and come back if you get stuck on a specific part.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now clearly articulated how the Asia/HK tension fits their argument — as both supporting and complicating evidence. This is sophisticated thinking. They have all the ingredients for a strong thesis and essay structure. Per the redundancy rule and conversation length (we're at exchange 8), I should now firmly push them toward writing their draft rather than continuing to discuss ideas.

exchanges.student: Ok yeah that helps, thanks — I think I can draft now. If I get stuck, I’ll ask abt the intro or structure.
exchanges.tutor: Good — go for it. When you come back, bring what you've written and we'll work through it together. I won't help with phrasing or structure directly, but I can ask questions that help you see where your argument needs strengthening.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrapping up and ready to draft. I should give a brief encouraging close while reminding them I'm here to guide, not write for them. I should also set clear expectations for when they return with a draft.

exchanges.student: Got it, I’ll try a draft first. I might need a quick check later if my argument gets too repetitive.
exchanges.tutor: Sounds good. When you're back, I'll help you spot repetition by asking you what each paragraph is actually adding to your argument. That's usually the quickest way to find it yourself. Good luck with the draft!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is closing the conversation on a positive note. I should briefly affirm and set expectations for the next interaction — specifically that I'll help them identify repetition through questions, not by editing for them.
